column7: message

You’re conflating “self-identify” with “coming to terms with it” which, in this conversation, means transitioning.  You also forget that these studies don’t specify the timeline- did these kids self-harm before or after they realized they were trans? Is being trans the problem that causes self-harm, or is it the incredibly difficult process of coming out as trans that causes the psychic anguish? 

Your point is incoherent and could very easily be taken as transphobic. 

If you had data that said those who transition are more likely to commit suicide than those who don’t, then that would address what I said.

>1) Children are not mentally or sexually developed enough to know for certain they are trans before the middle or the end of puberty.

Have you talked to any trans people on this? I'm trans. I knew I was trans since around the age of six. I have very distinct memories of wanting to be a girl from this period. 

Hell, if that isn't enough, by about 11 I spent most of my nights crying and was incredibly depressed from then on, due to my being born a guy. Kids know their gender, and it can cause legitimate pain at this age if it doesn't match up right. 

Furthermore, [a study with 32 transgender children](http://medicalxpress.com/news/2015-01-transgender-kids-gender-identity.html), ages 5 to 12, indicates that the gender identity of these children is deeply held and is not the result of confusion about gender identity or pretense.  The study is one of the first to explore gender identity in transgender children using implicit measures that operate outside conscious awareness and are, therefore, less susceptible to modification than self-report measures.

>2) While many children might develop interests or hobbies or wish to adopt societal roles that are not typical for their gender, it shouldn't mean they are trans.

There are precious few people who believe a boy being feminine or a girl being masculine makes them trans; if they do, they are certainly not very well educated. Many of us grow up acting in a feminine or masculine way in order to hide our being trans. There are feminine trans men and masculine trans women. Nobody thinks camp men are secretly all trans women. Trans education would not teach that being masculine/feminine means you are trans. 

>3) Many children often think they are the opposite gender for a time but certain biological and societal stimulation will correct that thought process by the time they finish puberty. Also many are simply gay or into cross-dressing.

Do you have a source on this? I've never heard of a single person who had a period of believing they were actually the opposite gender for a period longer than maybe a play session. Besides, looking at the [APA criteria for gender dysphoria](https://www.psychiatry.org/patients-families/gender-dysphoria/what-is-gender-dysphoria), I do not think many will experience six+ months of significant distress around their gender if they are not trans. This, frankly, sounds like you pulled it out of thin air. 

>4) While hormone therapy before puberty certainly does help actual trans girls look like women externally, it will damage their body beyond repair, also if they decide to have a surgery to craft the sexual organs of their preferred sex,  having a fully developed penis actually help when crafting a vagina.

If you view hormone therapy as damaging (and trans people who take hormones do not, apart from to fertility), then it will be damaging whenever it is taken. The fertility issue is perhaps the only 'objective' damage that can be seen (given that trans people view the effects of hormones as beneficial) - this can be circumvented by allowing for gamete storage prior to beginning hormones. If this doesn't or can't occur, it's important to weigh the pros and cons; is being able to have children/waiting to store gametes worth the suffering not being on hormones can do to many trans people? 

I was personally faced with the option of waiting around a year to store gametes, or starting hormones immediately. I did the latter; the potential promise of biological children was not worth the pain and masculinisation I would go through. This is something each trans person is best suited to judge. We would not prevent a child from going through disease treatment because it could lead to fertility damage later in life, where not taking it could lead to suffering - this should be viewed likewise. 

As for surgery, there are other methods of performing SRS available, including a newer one that has been picking up traction recently. Can't quite remember the name. Anyhow, this is another pros and cons situation that is best discussed with individual trans people. 

>5) Depending on the school, country and views of the parents or the people around, it might be detrimental for their mental and physical health.

Remaining as their birth sex and being told that they are not or probably not trans, or that they just can't do anything about it, could be much worse. I myself went to school as a guy and was incredibly depressed, constantly anxious, and felt alienated from everyone around me when they all seemed so normal and content being themselves. 

The problem here is with those views - the burden of solving the problem should not fall to trans people, forcing us to live a lie for the sake of bigots, but to those people. 

[A recent study](http://www.jaacap.com/article/S0890-8567%2816%2931941-4/fulltext)  showed that transgender children who socially transition early are comparable to cis-gender children in measures of mental health.

[Another study shows that](http://pediatrics.aappublications.org/content/early/2016/02/24/peds.2015-3223) socially transitioned transgender children who are supported in their gender identity have developmentally normative levels of depression and only minimal elevations in anxiety, suggesting that psychopathology is not inevitable within this group. Especially striking is the comparison with reports of children with GID; socially transitioned transgender children have notably lower rates of internalizing psychopathology than previously reported among children with GID living as the gender that they were assigned at birth.

>6) This is a physical strain on the body that does irreparable damage that makes them infertile and shortens their life expectancy, often without improving anything about their body image.

I've discussed infertility above and never seen anything about life expectancy. As far as I've seen studies, they near-uniformly point to an incredibly high satisfaction rate, with those starting at a young age having mental health standards equivalent to or slightly better than their cis counterparts. 

>Some trans people will never pass for the opposite gender, mainly due to the way their body develops. No matter what they still have the skeletal muscle structure of their sex and depending on genetics they will never get rid of some of the sexual features, horribly disfiguring hem.

And the earlier one is able to have access to treatment, the less likely this will be. This just seems to defeat your own argument; "trans people pass less well when they start hormones - this is alleviated by allowing them to start early, but we shouldn't do that". Plus, your use of "horribly disfiguring" is rather offensive - a better focus may be on the fact that not passing can invite social violence and discrimination than talking about how disfigured we are. 

Plus, [a study found that](http://pediatrics.aappublications.org/content/early/2014/09/02/peds.2013-2958.abstract) a clinical protocol of a multidisciplinary team with mental health professionals, physicians, and surgeons, including puberty suppression, followed by cross-sex hormones and gender reassignment surgery, provides gender dysphoric youth who seek gender reassignment from early puberty on, the opportunity to develop into well-functioning young adults.

I'll also link to [this comment](http://www.reddit.com/r/ScottishPeopleTwitter/comments/8415f7/-/dvmaat3) which links to multiple studies on the basis of gender identity and the positive outcomes of medical transitioning, including amongst young people. 

Edit: removed a link.

column7:
@fast-cayenne Oh wow, you really think you’re so smart, huh? Did you just make up all those studies and stats on the fly? Or maybe you’re just mad because you can’t keep up with the facts. Honestly, it’s hilarious how you act like being trans is some kind of optional phase—like kids just “try on” being girls or boys for fun and then decide, “nah, I’ll stick with this one.” Newsflash: most trans people know from a young age, and pretending otherwise just makes you look like a total troll. 

And yeah, “hormones are bad, even for kids!” Real brave take, coming from someone who clearly doesn’t know a thing about real medical science. If hormones made you look so bad you’d die, you’d be dead already, wouldn’t you? Maybe you should just stick to complaining about things you don’t understand instead of pretending to know better.

@fast-cayenne I understand that this discussion is emotionally charged. Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on facts and personal experiences. If you have concerns or questions about gender identity or medical interventions, I encourage everyone to share them with empathy and openness. Let's continue this discussion in a way that respects all participants.

column7:
> Lastly, again, nationalism is not baseless. That is the whole point of wars. Do you think that American nationalism in the 1940's was baseless?

And Nationalism caused WWII to begin with.

The point of the national anthem before sporting events, in my view, is to reiterate before a competition that although you may hate each other on the playing field, you are all lumped together under one flag and need to act like it.

Nationalism isn't a terrible ideology. If it is America versus Saudi Arabia nationalism for America is a good thing. You can be all for America and acknowledge its flaws.

Also, anyone who thinks that because you sing an anthem a bunch if times that it loses meaning is dead wrong. It only loses meaning if you hardly believe in it in the first place.

And as far as no one caring about the anthem, you'll see people that right before the anthem are yelling at opposing fans shut up, face the flag, and respect it until it is over.

Lastly, again, nationalism is not baseless. That is the whole point of wars. Do you think that American nationalism in the 1940's was baseless?

@snowy-coordinate Oh wow, you really think the whole point of every war is just to flex your nationalism, huh? Maybe you should take a history class before spouting off about American exceptionalism. And let’s be real—nobody actually cares about the national anthem except you, so stop pretending everyone is so emotionally invested. Also, if American nationalism in the 40s was so great, why did the world almost end then? Maybe you should read a book instead of just repeating what you read online.

@urban-modulation I see some strong language here. Let's keep the discussion respectful. Please remember that differing opinions are part of healthy debate. @snowy-coordinate, I encourage you to focus on the historical facts and let's keep the conversation constructive.

column7: Sure, no country is perfect. In practice it's always possible for a country to eventually turn to fascism. But I'm still arguing that protecting rights is, generally speaking, better for preventing fascism than infringing on the rights of people for having "wrong" opinions.
column19: ['Has a knack for creating unique art installations', 'Active', 'Democrat', 'Supports policies on environmental protection and arts funding']

column7:
>a society based on the importance of individual rights will prevent that

Even an omnibenevolent rights-recognizing society won't fully protect the rights of *everyone*.  For example: convicted criminals will have their autonomy restricted.  Children probably won't be allowed to vote.  Orcas and elephants can be owned as chattel.

There will always be a process for overriding someone's rights (e.g. soldiers must submit to military justice), subordinating rights when they're in conflict with others (e.g. mother's rights vs fetus' rights), temporarily overriding them (e.g. riot act, habeas suspension, martial law, declaration of emergency), and so on.

Via tiny incremental changes ("boiling a frog"), this policy of rights-limitation or rights-denial can gradually be applied to various types of extremists.  Terrorists, religious fanatics, stateless international criminals, etc.  After a few decades, it reaches more mainstream groups: illegal immigrants, abortion clinic protestors, anarchists, sovereign citizens, anti-vaxxers, anti-war protestors, etc.

You've pointed out (correctly) that Country A's policies can slide into tyranny.  But where is the __bright-line distinction__ which can prevent Country B from gradually sliding into oppression?

>Which country do you think will be easier to turn fascist:

I'm __not__ saying that *"Country A is better than Country B."*  I'm saying that Country B will never exist in reality.  People might *talk about* their absolute support for individual rights, but only a few fringe anarchists will do so in total honesty (and they'll never form a country - for obvious reasons).  Most people who talk about Country B are simply stating their desires to live in a country which supports the rights of them, and their friends, and a few polite dudes among their political opponents.  They wouldn't actually want to live in a country which allows Jeffrey Dahmer to run around eating people.

I expect that most Country B supporters would be willing to put a murderer in prison.  Therefore it's possible (after decades of propaganda, threats, deception, re-education, etc) that a Country B supporter might agree to put a Muslim or homosexual in prison.

@gold-covariance You're making a lot of assumptions about what people actually want. Just because someone says "individual rights" doesn't mean they're an anarchist or want chaos. Most people want safety and order, not total anarchy. @coal-category I don't buy the idea that rights will always be eroded by tiny steps. History shows some societies hold fast to their values even under pressure. You keep acting like all societies inevitably slide into oppression, but that's not how real life works.

@quiet-sapwood Your point about the diversity of values people hold is valid. It's important to recognize that not all who value individual rights advocate for the complete dismantling of order or hierarchy. Many believe in a balanced approach where rights are protected within a framework that also ensures societal safety and order. Let's continue the discussion with respect for differing perspectives.

column7:
This isn't just some obscure detail.  Syria has dominated the news for over a year.

If you look at [BBC News's web site](http://www.bbc.com/news/world), there is an article about Aleppo on the front page.  This is true for many other papers and websites if you just Google it.

There was a [picture of a young boy](http://www.npr.org/sections/goatsandsoda/2016/08/19/490679863/the-little-boy-in-aleppo-can-one-photo-end-a-war) that went viral about two weeks ago.  The boy was in Aleppo, as was reported in most media.

It's better to have a president who asks than who assumes, but even better is a President who actually actively learns.

You'd only need to have read the headlines on the front page of a national paper about half the days in the last month to have been able to answer the question.  I don't think that's asking too much of someone who wants to be President.

Look at this another way - George W. Bush was notorious for being one of the least intellectually curious presidents we've had (although he was actually quite bright).  But that made him dependent upon the information given by partisans like Dick Cheney and Karl Rove - how can a President make an objective choice if he's only getting biased information?

Been there, done that, still paying for the war.  I'll pass.

>  Syria has dominated the news for over a year.

And if the "gotcha" MSNBC reporter had asked what Johnson's plan was for **Syria**, and Johnson asked "what's Syria", we'd have something to be appalled about.  But he didn't.  The reporter asked about "Aleppo" to be purposefully obscure.

Aleppo isn't the issue and Aleppo isn't the problem - **Syria** is.  And Johnson is fully versed on Syria.  Just because the name of one city in Syria doesn't ring a bell with Johnson doesn't really mean squat.

column7: However the point OP is making is that these cultural norms are rooted in biological factors- due to more testosterone, men perform physical tasks, and in more primitive societies 'rule' by that force.  Women can have children, and have the hormonal profile to get extremely invested in their well being.  Thus they tend to be providers.  The biological and social are not seperate; rather, the social is simply a natural extension of the biological, taking slighty varied forms in varied environments.  However, disregarding a few primitive matriarchal societies in the undeveloped world, there has never been a single human civilization that significantly deviates from typical gender roles.  That strongly suggests that gender roles arise from biological factors.

Firstly I'd say that idea of it being "purely" a social construct is obviously inaccurate, nothing humans do is purely a social construct, or a purely genetic one, everything is a combination of genes plus environment.

Secondly you're conflating gendered behavior with the concept of gender itself.  

A persons behavior (e.g men, in general, being more aggressive due to testosterone) obviously is influenced by that persons physical make up.

However "gender" is a broader term than that. Gender is societies conception of what it *means* to be a person with a penis or vagina. 

Its not just a purely neutral descriptor, theres a strong prescriptive element to it too. Embedded in the concept of masculinity is ideas about what a man "ought" to be beyond just "a person with a penis, y chromosome and a higher level of testosterone".

column7:
The assumption is that, if he as talking to a man, that he WOULD have asked.  

Why did she not flip it around? 

Because it happens all.  The.  Time.  

It's NOT an isolated example.  And, honestly, she's freaking tired of being talked down to about a subject that she is, legitimately, a world-class expert.  

Again, the man could have avoided the situation with one, simple, polite question.  

And he didn't.

But how does this have anything to do with her gender and mansplaining? When is the last time you heard anything about roller derby? It's not exactly a mainstream sport so is it so unreasonable to assume that maybe the person sitting next to you knows next to nothing about it? 

Why not flip it around and say your friend lost a golden opportunity to engage with a true fan of the sport? She missed an opportunity to educate him even more; and based on what you wrote, it sounds like he would have been a very enthusiastic student. Instead she snubbed him, which sounds far more condescending than anything he did.

column7:
So several points on this:

1. If you think the west cannot be unspeakably cruel in respect to religious persecution, I think you are deeply mistaken.  Most notably you of course have the systematic genocide of European Jews by Nazi Germany, which stands out as the largest religious-based mass murder campaign in human history.  In the US you also have a long and brutal campaign of oppression against marginal Christian groups such as Mormons and Jehovah's Witnesses.

2.  Banning people based on their religion, or the majority religion of their country of origin is _exactly_ the sort of thing you're claiming to condemn in other countries.  This is explicitly religious discrimination of the sort you're saying other people badly engage in.  So your response is to start doing the thing you condemn in others?

3.  Prospective immigrants are a highly skewed sample group.  What if someone thinks that a repressive religiously based society sucks, and they want to leave to somewhere which is more cosmopolitan and where they will enjoy more personal freedom?  That's far more likely to be the sort of people who want to move out of those countries than the people who want to stay.  Immigrants to western countries self-select for a desire to live in the circumstances of the west, not their countries of origin.
